title: What the Body Does at the Holiday Table: Listening Beyond the Feast
dek: You sit at the holiday table, the familiar clatter of plates and hum of voices pressing against your ribcage. Your jaw clenches - masseter tight as a drawn bowstring - and your diaphragm catches in a slow, shallow hitch. This is not weakness or failure; this is the body speaking in its own language. Here, in the swirl of family, food, and festering histories, your body remembers ancient survival patterns, the unspoken rules about fury, and the secret medicine of roots and breath.
bodyMarkdown:
You sit at the holiday table, the air thick with the scent of roasted turkey, cinnamon, and pine. The clock reads 7:03 pm, the sun already bowed out to a cold December dusk, the sky a steel-gray canvas. You notice the subtle tightening in your jaw  -  the masseter muscles clenched almost imperceptibly, as if bracing for impact. The body does not lie. Your trapezius muscles hitch up toward your ears; your diaphragm catches in a shallow inhale that never quite fills your lungs. The levator scapulae in your neck stiffen, pulling your head slightly forward, the way you do when you brace for a disagreeable word or a remembered slight. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body remembers what the mind tries to suppress.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.184Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

## The Jaw Remembers: Muscular Memory Around the Table

Susan Drury often reminds the women she works with that their fury was never ugly  -  it was survival. And survival is written in the fibers of the body, especially in the muscles that clench when you feel unseen or unheard. At the holiday table, the masseter  -  the powerful muscle responsible for chewing  -  tightens in response not just to food but to old patterns of family dynamics. You might not notice it consciously, but the jaw remembers the years you were told to "be nice," to "smile," to "not make a fuss." 

The masseter’s chronic tension can radiate down to the temporomandibular joint, causing subtle aches or a sense of being trapped in your own head. The levator scapulae, which lifts the shoulder blade, tightens when you carry the weight of anticipation  -  the dread of a certain conversation or the expectation of silence that feels like a cage. When the diaphragm, the primary muscle of breath, constricts, it signals a fight-or-flight response. You may find your breathing shallow and rapid, as if the body is preparing to defend itself, even though you’re sitting still.

These muscles and their tensions are the body’s way of speaking. They are a somatic message: you are on alert, you are holding back, you are readying for the old stories to replay.

## The Body Knows: How Embodied Awareness Reveals Hidden Stories

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen master and teacher, teaches that "the body knows how to breathe, how to relax, how to heal." But only when you slow down enough to listen. At the holiday table, this listening is radical. It requires you to slow your breath, soften your jaw, and feel the weight of your shoulders drop  -  even if just for a moment.

You might notice the rough texture of the wooden table beneath your fingertips, the slight chill of the air conditioning mixing with the warmth of the room. The scent of dried sage from the centerpiece drifts near your nose, grounding you in the present even as your mind races through old arguments and future anxieties. Here, in this somatic presence, you create a crack in the armor of habitual tension.

An embodied practice inspired by Thich Nhat Hanh’s mindfulness invites you to place one hand on your belly and the other on your heart. Breathe slowly in through the nose, feeling the diaphragm expand  -  the muscle descending and the lungs filling with air. Exhale through the mouth, sensing the release of the trapezius and levator scapulae as the shoulders drop. Repeat this three times, savoring the tactile feedback of your body’s movement. This practice doesn’t erase the tension but makes it visible, palpable, and therefore negotiable.

## Herbs as Medicine: Allies for the Body’s Stress Signals

Herbal medicine is not about magic; it’s about respecting the chemistry of plants and their relationship to our biology. When the body is tense at the holiday table, herbs like chamomile and skullcap can gently ease the nervous system, inviting the diaphragm to relax and the breath to deepen. The bitter root of valerian works on the central nervous system, reducing the fight-or-flight response encoded in the fascia that surrounds the muscles.

Susan Drury often recommends tinctures such as [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) taken before the meal, especially when the body is already tight from anticipation. These herbs do not numb or dull; they support the body’s natural ability to regulate itself. The fascia, the connective tissue enveloping muscles like the masseter and trapezius, responds to this gentle chemical conversation by loosening its grip.

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) offers a rich vocabulary for understanding this tension. The liver qi stagnation, often described as the body’s anger or frustration stuck in place, manifests as tightness along the ribs and shoulders. Herbs such as bupleurum and angelica root are used in formulas to move this qi, allowing the emotional energy to flow instead of becoming trapped and painful.

## Family Ties and Body Memory: The Legacy in the Psoas

The psoas muscle, deep in the abdomen, is often called the body’s core or its “fight muscle.” Resmaa Menakem, a leading somatic therapist focusing on racialized trauma, emphasizes the psoas as a repository of intergenerational trauma, a muscle that tenses when we anticipate threat  -  not only from external sources but from the echoes of ancestral pain.

At the holiday table, you may feel your psoas gripping tightly, pulling your spine forward as if ready to flee or fight. This contraction is a primal response to the unspoken tensions in the room  -  the weight of family expectations, the history of conflict, the desire to both connect and protect yourself. This is not pathology; this is survival biology.

Feeling the psoas contract is often experienced as a sinking or tightening in the lower belly, a subtle but persistent pressure. It can manifest as restlessness, a sense of needing to move or escape. Recognizing this tension in the psoas is the first step toward offering it permission to soften.


## The Breath as Bridge: Pema Chödrön’s Invitation to Difficulty

Pema Chödrön, the American Buddhist nun and teacher, encourages us to "lean into the sharp points" of our experience. At the holiday table, this means using the breath as a bridge into discomfort rather than away from it. The breath moves through the diaphragm, the levator scapulae, and the trapezius, connecting the head to the heart and the heart to the gut.

When you find yourself bracing, notice where the breath is stuck. Is it caught high in the chest, shallow and quick? Or is it held low in the belly, as if the psoas muscle is guarding it? Pema’s teaching is to welcome these sensations as part of your body’s conversation, not something to fear or avoid.

Try this: inhale slowly to a count of four, imagining the breath traveling down into the belly and the psoas. Hold gently for a count of two, then exhale to a count of six, allowing the muscles around the ribs and shoulders to soften. Repeat this cycle five times, each exhale an invitation to let go a little more.

This is not about forcing relaxation; it’s about listening to your body’s rhythm and responding with kindness. The body knows what to do  -  you are simply the steward of the process.

## The Smell of the Table: Sensory Details That Ground or Trigger

The olfactory nerves connect directly to the limbic system, the brain’s emotional center. This means the smells at the holiday table are deeply charged: the pine from the wreath, the sharp citrus of cranberry sauce, the warmth of cinnamon and cloves in the mulled wine. These scents can soothe or ignite old memories.

You might notice the subtle bitterness of burnt edges on the roasted vegetables, the oily residue of gravy on your fingers, or the powdery sweetness of nutmeg dusted on the custard. These sensations mingle with the body’s tension, sometimes exacerbating the sense of overwhelm.

Herbal allies here include rosemary and peppermint, which can be used as essential oils applied to the temples or wrists to clear the mind and ease the chest. The aroma of lavender, long cherished for its calming properties, can be diffused in the room or carried in a sachet to help modulate the nervous system.

The body’s response to these sensory inputs is immediate and often unconscious, a reminder that the holiday experience is as much about what is felt as what is said.

## When the Table Becomes a Battlefield: Recognizing and Respecting Your Limits

Robert Augustus Masters, a pioneer in somatic psychotherapy, writes extensively about the importance of boundaries and the bodily experience of shame and anger. At the holiday table, when old wounds are reopened and voices rise, the body sends signals: a tightening in the throat, a clutching sensation in the solar plexus, the rise of heat in the face.

These are not signs of weakness but of your body’s fierce desire to protect your integrity. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles might seize up, the masseter grind tightly, and the psoas lock down  -  all to shield you from emotional harm.

Honoring these signals means you have permission to excuse yourself, to breathe deeply, to step outside and feel the cold air on your face, to sip a calming herbal tea like lemon balm or holy basil. It means recognizing that your anger is a messenger, not a moral failing, and that your body’s wisdom is a guide to self-care.

## The Aftermath: What the Body Needs When the Feast Ends

The holiday table eventually clears, the dishes piled high, the room quieter but no less charged. The body, having held tension for hours, now needs restoration. The diaphragm, often short-circuited by anxiety, benefits from gentle stretching and mindful breathing. The trapezius and levator scapulae, tight from vigilance, need slow, deliberate movements to encourage fascia release.

Herbal baths infused with dried lavender, chamomile, and rose petals invite the nervous system to soften. A warm compress applied to the neck and shoulders can ease the levator scapulae’s clenched grip. Taking time to rest in a supported posture, perhaps with a bolster beneath the knees to relax the psoas, allows the body to reset.

Gabor Maté’s work on trauma underscores the importance of compassionate self-attunement after stress. The body has done the work of survival; now it requires care and acknowledgment. This is not indulgence. This is necessity.

## FAQ

**Q: Why does my jaw clench so hard at family meals?**

**A:** Your masseter muscles clench as a protective response, often unconsciously. The jaw remembers years of holding back fury or bracing against emotional discomfort. This tension is a biological message signaling that your body is on alert.

**Q: Can herbs really help with holiday stress?**

**A:** Yes. Herbs like chamomile, valerian, and skullcap have long been used to soothe the nervous system and relax muscle tension. They support your body’s natural ability to regulate stress without numbing your feelings.

**Q: What is the psoas, and why does it matter?**

**A:** The psoas is a deep core muscle involved in fight-or-flight responses. It holds tension related to trauma and stress, including ancestral pain. Recognizing its tightness can help you understand your body’s emotional state and begin to release long-held tension.

**Q: How can I use breath to manage tension during difficult conversations?**

**A:** Slow, mindful breathing that engages the diaphragm helps regulate your nervous system. Practices inspired by teachers like Pema Chödrön invite you to welcome discomfort with kindness, using the breath as a bridge through tension.

**Q: What should I do if I feel overwhelmed at the holiday table?**

**A:** Honor your body’s signals by stepping away if possible, breathing deeply, and using herbal allies like lemon balm or holy basil. Remember that your anger is a messenger, and caring for your body is essential.

The holiday table is more than a place to eat; it is a stage where your body, memory, and family stories play out in muscle, breath, and scent. To listen deeply is to honor not just the feast but the fierce, beautiful life force within you.

## The Fascia Remembers: The Connective Tissue of Family History

Beneath the skin, beneath the muscle, lies the fascia - a continuous web of connective tissue that wraps every organ, every nerve, every muscle fiber in your body. It is the body’s internal scaffolding, and it is highly responsive to emotional stress. When you sit at the holiday table and the conversation turns to old grievances, your fascia tightens. It loses its fluidity, becoming rigid and sticky, binding your muscles in a protective sheath. This is not a metaphor; it is a physiological response to perceived threat. The fascia remembers the shape of your childhood fear, the posture of your adolescent defiance, the exact angle of your shoulders when you were told to be quiet and smile.

Thomas Myers, a pioneer in the study of fascial anatomy, describes how emotional trauma can become locked in the fascial web, creating patterns of tension that persist long after the initial event has passed. At the holiday table, you are not just reacting to the present moment; you are navigating a complex web of historical tension held in your very connective tissue. When your aunt makes that familiar, passive-aggressive comment about your life choices, it is not just your mind that reacts. Your superficial fascia, just beneath the skin of your neck and chest, tightens like a drumhead.

You can feel this tension if you pay attention. It is a subtle, pervasive tightness, a feeling of being bound or restricted. It is the body’s way of creating armor, of solidifying its boundaries when emotional boundaries are being breached. This is biology, not pathology. Your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do: protect you from harm. But this protection comes at a cost. The rigid fascia restricts your breathing, limits your movement, and keeps your nervous system locked in a state of high alert.

## The Vagus Nerve and the Social Engagement System

Stephen Porges, the developer of Polyvagal Theory, has mapped the intricate pathways of the vagus nerve, the wandering nerve that connects your brain to your heart, lungs, and gut. This nerve is the primary conduit of your parasympathetic nervous system, responsible for rest, digestion, and social engagement. At the holiday table, your vagus nerve is working overtime, constantly assessing the environment for cues of safety or danger.

When the atmosphere is tense, when the voices around the table grow sharp or dismissive, your vagus nerve detects the threat. It shifts your nervous system out of the social engagement state and into a defensive posture. Your heart rate increases, your digestion slows down, and your facial muscles - the ones responsible for expressing empathy and connection - become rigid. You might find it difficult to make eye contact, or your voice might lose its natural modulation, becoming flat or strained.

This is the body’s ancient survival mechanism at work. It is prioritizing immediate safety over social connection. You are not being difficult or antisocial; your biology is simply responding to the cues it is receiving. The challenge is to find ways to signal safety to your vagus nerve, even in the midst of a challenging environment. This is where somatic practices and herbal allies become essential tools for navigating the holiday table.

## The Medicine of Bitter Roots

In traditional herbalism, bitter herbs are revered for their ability to stimulate digestion and move stagnant energy. They are the medicine of the liver, the organ associated with anger and frustration in Traditional Chinese Medicine. When you suppress your anger at the holiday table, when you swallow your words and force a smile, that energy does not simply disappear. It stagnates, creating heat and tension in the body.

Bitter roots like dandelion and burdock are profound allies in this process. They remind the body how to process and eliminate what it no longer needs. A tincture of [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) taken before the holiday meal can help prepare your digestive system for the heavy food, but it also prepares your energetic system to process the heavy emotions. The bitter taste on your tongue is a wake-up call to your vagus nerve, a signal to engage the parasympathetic nervous system and begin the work of digestion - both physical and emotional.

You might also consider [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20), a deep-acting alterative that helps clear the blood and support the liver. It is a grounding herb, drawing your energy down into your roots, away from the swirling chaos of the mind and the tense upper body. When you feel your energy rising into your chest and throat, when your jaw clenches and your breath becomes shallow, the medicine of bitter roots can help anchor you back into your center.

## The Pelvic Floor: The Foundation of Safety

We rarely talk about the pelvic floor in polite company, let alone at the holiday table. But this complex hammock of muscles at the base of your pelvis is intimately connected to your sense of safety and survival. It is the root of your physical structure, and it is often the first place the body registers fear or threat.

When you feel attacked or undermined by family members, your pelvic floor may instinctively contract, pulling your energy inward and upward in a protective gesture. This contraction is often unconscious, a subtle tightening that you might only notice if you deliberately bring your attention to it. Over time, chronic tension in the pelvic floor can lead to a host of physical issues, from lower back pain to digestive disturbances.

Bringing awareness to your pelvic floor is a radical act of self-reclamation. It is a way of saying to your body, "I am here, I am listening, and I am safe." You can practice this awareness even while sitting at the table. Notice the contact between your sit bones and the chair. Imagine the muscles of your pelvic floor softening and widening with each exhale, releasing their grip on your survival energy. This simple act of somatic attention can profoundly shift your experience of the holiday gathering, grounding you in your own physical reality and reminding you that you have the power to choose how you respond.
